content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P O BOX 407 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO 95

August 19, 1977
77-298

Edward F. Smith, Attorney
Governmental Affairs Division
The Du Pont Company
Legal Department, Room 7029
Du Pont Building
Wilmington, Delaware 19898

Re: Opinion Request
No. 77-014

Dear Mr. Smith:

Thank you for your letter of May 10, 1977, in
which you request an opinion with respect to the reporting
provisions of the Political Reform Act. Since your letter
does not raise a substantial question under the Act, no
formal opinion will be issued. However, I hope the following
informal comments will be helpful.

You ask whether the Du Pont Company can underwrite
the expenses involved in flying a member of an organic solvents
subcommittee of the California Air Resources Board from
San Diego, California, to a Du Pont Company installation in
Troy, Michigan. The purpose of the trip is to give the
subcommittee member a first-hand view of the operations of a
"refinish spray booth." The organic solvents subcommittee
is an advisory task force established to assist the ARB
staff in the drafting of regulations. The member in question
sits as an unsalaried member of the advisory subcommittee
and is a full-time employee of the San Diego County Air
Pollution Control Board. Although he occasionally receives
travel expenses from the state, he is not a state employee.

Specifically, you ask whether the Political Reform
Act prohibits Du Pont from providing free transportation and
accommodations to the subcommittee member for the purpose of
permitting him to view the spray booth in Michigan. As we
explain more fully below, the Act does not prohibit Du Pont
from underwriting these travel expenses. The Act does require, however, that the payments be reported properly by
Du Pont on its Lobbyist Employer Statement (Form 650) and by

Edward F. Smith, Attorney
August 19, 1977
Page Two


the subcommittee official on his Statement of Economic Interests. Furthermore, if the trip is considered a gift to the official, the official may be required to disqualify himself from participating in decisions made by the San Diego County Air Pollution Control Board concerning Du Pont. No disqualification would be required, however, in connection with the official's duties on the advisory subcommittee to the State Air Resources Board. I will develop each of these points.

The possibility that payment for the trip by Du Pont arises from Government Code Section 86203 1/ of the Act which bars a lobbyist from making, arranging, or acting as an agent or intermediary in the making of a gift of $10 or more in a calendar month to a state "agency official." This prohibition, however, does not apply to the instant case. An unsalaried member of an advisory board is not an "agency official" within the meaning of the Act (see opinion requested by John C. Morrissey, 2 FPPC Ops. 84 (No. 75-099, July 6, 1976)). The Air Resources Board advises us that the organic solvents subcommittee is strictly an advisory committee with no decision making functions. Therefore, no prohibitions apply, and not only is it permissible for Du Pont to pay for the trip, but it would also be permissible for a lobbyist to be involved in arranging the trip.

It is required, however, that the payments involved be properly reported by both Du Pont and the official. Since it is the employer of a lobbyist, Du Pont must file a Form 650 disclosing all "payments to influence legislative or administrative action" (Section 86109). We request the payments for this trip as "payments ... in connection with direct communication with any ... agency official" within the meaning of Section 82045(d), and they should therefore be reported on part 2, schedule i of Du Pont's Form 650. We reach this conclusion because, although the subcommittee member is not himself an "agency official," he can be expected to communicate with agency officials of the Air Resources Board as a result of his trip. Thus, "direct communication" within the meaning of Section 82045(d) would occur here.

With respect to the subcommittee member, he may have to disclose receipt of the trip as a gift on the Statement of Economic Interests he files in connection with his employment

_____________
1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted.

Edward F. Smith, Attorney
August 19, 1977
Page Three


on the San Diego Air Pollution Control Board.2/ Moreover,
regardless of whether the official has a disclosure obligation,
if the trip is a gift to him, he may be required to disqualify
himself from participating in decisions made by the San Diego
Air Pollution Control Board respecting Du Pont.

Therefore, two questions are raised. First, what
portion of a trip such as this is a "gift"? Second, to the
extent the trip is a gift, would it be a gift to the official
or would it be a gift to San Diego County,3/ thereby relieving
the official from any disclosure or disqualification obligations?

With respect to the first question, the cost of
the transportation from San Diego to Troy and payments for
the official's food, accommodation and entertainment during
his stay in Troy would be a gift. Any costs incurred in
connection with the official's tour and inspection of Du Pont's
facilities, however, would not be a gift since the Commission
has concluded that such costs are in the nature of "informational material." "Informational material" is specifically
excluded from the Act's definition of "gift." (Section
82028; see opinion requested by John Spellman, 1 FPPC Ops.
16 (No. 75-026, May 1, 1975).)

To the extent a gift is involved, the next
question is whether the gift is to the official or to San Diego
County. The Commission has held in a recent opinion that if
certain criteria are met, a benefit received by an official
in connection with his official duties will be considered a
gift to his government employer. In such a case, the official
does not have to disclose receipt of the gift on his Statement
of Economic Interests and need not disqualify himself from

2/ The subcommittee member has no disclosure
obligations with respect to his service on the Air Resources
Board since he serves in a purely advisory capacity. However,
he may have disclosure obligations in connection with his
San Diego County employment if the San Diego County Air
Pollution Control Board now has a conflict of interest code
in effect and if the code requires the official to disclose
gifts of this type.

3/ We are advised by the Air Resources Board that
if the official sought reimbursement for this trip, he would
have to apply to his employer, the San Diego Air Pollution
Control Board. He would not be eligible for reimbursement
from the state. Therefore, if any government entity receives
a gift as a result of this transaction, it would be the
County of San Diego.

Edward P. Smith, Attorney
August 19, 1977
Page Four


decisions concerning the donor of the gift.4/  See opinion
requested by Peter G. Stone, 3 FPPC Ops. 47, 51-53 (No. 77-
003, June 9, 1977), a copy of which I have enclosed.

     Applying the standards set forth in the Stone
opinion to your case, the official would not have to report
receipt of the trip and would not have to disqualify himself
from decisions concerning Du Pont if Du Pont makes the trip
available for use by San Diego County without specifying
whom the recipient should be, leaving that decision to the
County. Since out-of-state travel by employees of San Diego
County must be approved by the County Board of Supervisors,
the trip must be described in and accepted by a public re-
solution adopted by that body. If those standards are met,
the trip would be considered a gift to the government entity
rather than the official.

     Even if the standards are not met, however, and
the official is considered to have received a gift, he would
only have possible disqualification obligations with respect
to the decisions he makes on the San Diego Air Pollution
Control Board.5/  He would not be required to disqualify
himself from participation in any activities of the organic
solvents subcommittee to the State Air Resources Board since
he serves in only an advisory capacity on that board.

     I trust the foregoing answers your question. If
you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion request, you
may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321. I

___________________________

     4/  This discussion assumes that the official is
not provided with goods or services over and above those
ordinarily necessary for a trip of this nature. If he is,
these benefits would be gifts to the official even if his
plane fare, food and lodging are not because they are con-
sidered to be gifts to his employer.

     5/  The official would be required to disqualify
himself if he receives gifts worth $250 or more from Du Pont
during the 12 months preceding the decision in question and
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on Du Pont, distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally. Sections 87100 and
87103.

Edward F. Smith, Attorney
August 19, 1977
Page Five


apologize for the delay in responding to your letter but
appreciate the cooperation you have given our staff in de-
veloping the factual background of your inquiry. If we can
be of further assistance, please do not hesitate to call me
or Ted Prim, an attorney in the Commission's Legal Division.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:MJB:plh
Enclosure

Request # No. _______ U (

Date Received__________

Response Due__________

DU PONT DE NEMOURS & COMPANY

WILMINGTON DELAWARE 19898

U.S.A. DEPARTMENT                                                MAY 16, 1977

REGISTERED MAIL
RETURN RECEIPT REQUESTED


Mr. John C. Greenwood
California Fair Political Practices
Committee
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Mr. Greenwood:

The Du Pont Company is requesting a formal ruling or opinion from the Fair Political Practices Committee on the following:

Under the California Political Reform Act of 1974 and/or related statutes, is there any reason why the Du Pont Company cannot/should not underwrite the expenses involved in flying a member of an organic solvents subcommittee of the California Air Resources Board from San Diego, California, to a Du Pont Company installation in Troy, Michigan, for the purpose of giving this subcommittee member a firsthand view and detailed commentary on the operations of a "refinish spray booth"? What, if any, legal considerations may be involved?

Since the FPPC is charged with the responsibility of administering the relevant statutory law which might come into play in such an eventuality, would it be possible to have your earliest reply to this question(s).

Thank you very much, in anticipation of your prompt response to this inquiry.

Very truly yours,


Edward F. Smith
Attorney
Governmental Affairs Division

EFS/clg

sections.conclusion: The Act does not prohibit Du Pont from underwriting these travel expenses. However, the Act requires that the payments be reported properly by Du Pont on its Lobbyist Employer Statement (Form 650) and by the subcommittee official on his Statement of Economic Interests. Furthermore, if the trip is considered a gift to the official, the official may be required to disqualify himself from participating in decisions made by the San Diego County Air Pollution Control Board concerning Du Pont. No disqualification would be required in connection with the official's duties on the advisory subcommittee to the State Air Resources Board.
sections.question_synthetic: Can Du Pont Company legally pay for travel expenses to transport a California Air Resources Board advisory subcommittee member to view Du Pont facilities in Michigan under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Du Pont may pay for the trip because the subcommittee member is not an "agency official" under the Act, but the payments must be properly reported on Du Pont's Form 650 and the official's Statement of Economic Interests. The official may face disqualification requirements for San Diego County Air Pollution Control Board decisions if the trip constitutes a gift, though specific criteria can classify it as a gift to the government entity rather than the individual.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (dated May 10, 1977 referenced, with apparent duplicate May 10 letter shown) and the FPPC response (dated August 19, 1977). FPPC states 'no formal opinion will be issued' but provides detailed informal comments. OCR quality is moderate with some character recognition issues but extractable. Multiple footnotes provide important clarifications on disqualification requirements and gift characterization.

CONCLUSION: The Act does not prohibit Du Pont from underwriting these travel expenses. However, the Act requires that the payments be reported properly by Du Pont on its Lobbyist Employer Statement (Form 650) and by the subcommittee official on his Statement of Economic Interests. Furthermore, if the trip is considered a gift to the official, the official may be required to disqualify himself from participating in decisions made by the San Diego County Air Pollution Control Board concerning Du Pont. No disqualification would be required in connection with the official's duties on the advisory subcommittee to the State Air Resources Board.

Since your letter does not raise a substantial question under the Act, no formal opinion will be issued. However, I hope the following informal comments will be helpful. You ask whether the Du Pont Company can underwrite the expenses involved in flying a member of an organic solvents subcommittee of the California Air Resources Board from San Diego, California, to a Du Pont Company installation in Troy, Michigan. embedding.summary: FPPC informal opinion denying a formal opinion but providing guidance that Du Pont Company may legally underwrite travel expenses for a California Air Resources Board advisory subcommittee member to visit Du Pont facilities in Michigan, subject to proper reporting and potential gift/disqualification considerations.
